topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My breath hitched, the air suddenly thick and hard to draw in. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling so violently I had to pause before reading the subject line: "Regarding your recent piece on urban planning." My eyes scanned the text, and a cold, nauseating dread began to pool in my stomach. The article, the words I had poured my soul into, were attributed to 'Elias Vance.' My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming beneath my ribs. I reread the signature, the unfamiliar name, the jarring disconnect between my creative effort and this stranger's credit. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to pace, to run until my legs gave out, anything to shake off the sickening realization that someone else was profiting from my work.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the glow of the monitor reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes. Every word felt like a foreign object, alien to my own hands. A slow, creeping numbness started in my fingertips and radiated outward, leaving me feeling strangely hollowed out. It wasn't just the plagiarism; it was the sheer audacity of it. I pressed my palms flat against the cool desk, trying to anchor myself against the sudden vertigo. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles protesting. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat, a sound that felt too loud in the quiet office. How could they have done this? The injustice of it settled over me, heavy and suffocating, making my vision swim slightly at the edges.

[story 3]

When I finally looked up from the document, the room seemed to tilt. My hands, resting on the keyboard, were slick with a sudden clamminess. I slowly pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding deafeningly loud. I walked to the window, pulling the blinds aside just enough to see the indifferent cityscape outside. A wave of hot shame washed over me, making my cheeks burn. It felt like being exposed, stripped bare under harsh, unforgiving light. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image of the article—my voice, my analysis—out of my mind, but it only looped, a relentless, unwelcome echo in the hollow space where my confidence used to reside. I took a deep, shuddering breath, tasting only dust and panic.
